elements.sent-2411-010: In an increasingly data-driven economy, however, information is acquired through endogenous contracting with brokers who gather, process, and sell information.
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, employers hire pre-employment testing agencies to assess job candidates, consumers purchase product reviews from platforms like Consumer Reports and CarFax, and homebuyers pay for home inspections and appraisals.
elements.sent-2411-012: These information brokers typically have no intrinsic preferences over the agent's match in the search market but aim instead to maximize their profit.
elements.sent-2411-013: Still, information brokers play a role in shaping search outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-014: The amount of information they reveal to the agent can persuade him to stop or continue his search, and it therefore affects the creation and division of surplus in the market.
elements.sent-2411-015: Relatively little is known about how search outcomes are affected by a market for information, or how these outcomes vary with the market structure for information brokers.
elements.sent-2411-016: Yet, the answers to such questions may be of practical import.
elements.sent-2411-017: For instance, between 2013 and 2016, 469 car dealerships were engaged in litigation against CarFax for anti-competitive practices.
elements.sent-2411-018: The plaintiffs alleged that CarFax was a monopoly, controlling 90\% of the market for certifying pre-owned vehicles, and that CarFax improperly limited the ability of car dealers to seek information from other sources.
elements.sent-2411-019: While CarFax admitted to its monopoly status, it argued that its use of short-term contracts with dealers (rather than long-term exclusive agreements) was welfare improving because it incentivized CarFax and its competitors to improve information quality.\footnote{See details on the case Maxon Hyundai Mazda et al. v Carfax, Inc and the associated arguments referenced above at \url{https://casetext.com/case/mazda-v-carfax and \url{https://casetext.com/case/mazda-v-carfax-inc-1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: This paper explores the merit of such claims: how does competition, or lack thereof, among information brokers affect search outcomes, market efficiency, and the welfare of the searching agent? To this end, we embed a repeated contracting game between an agent and information brokers (henceforth, ``principals") into a canonical sequential search model \citep{mcc70 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The agent (``he") has a unit demand, and in each period, samples one good after incurring a search flow cost.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, unlike in the canonical search model, he does not observe the good's quality.
elements.sent-2411-023: Instead, each principal (``she") designs a signal of the good's quality and sets a price for it, and the agent chooses which signal to purchase, if any.
elements.sent-2411-024: The agent then observes a signal realization based on his purchase decision, and decides either to terminate his search by matching with the good, or to discard the good and move to the next period.
elements.sent-2411-025: We characterize the set of equilibrium payoffs in this persuaded search game and study how the equilibrium payoff set varies between monopolistic and competitive settings.
elements.sent-2411-026: Our findings align with conventional wisdom that competition among principals benefits the agent.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, our results challenge the prevailing view that competitive settings are more efficient compared to monopolistic ones; instead, we find that competition actually reduces the total surplus generated from search.
elements.sent-2411-028: These findings suggest nuanced implications for the regulation of dynamic markets for information.
elements.sent-2411-029: Should a regulator ``break up" a principal with a monopoly over information sales, like CarFax, into rival firms? Our results indicate the answer to this question depends on the goals of the regulator: If the regulator seeks to maximize efficiency, it will allow the monopolist to persist.
elements.sent-2411-030: If the regulator instead seeks to maximize the agent's surplus, it will break up the monopolist.
elements.sent-2411-031: At the heart of our main result is the insight that the agent's willingness-to-pay for a signal is determined not only by the signal's informativeness, as would be the case in a static setting, but also by the informativeness and the prices of future signals if he continues searching.
elements.sent-2411-032: In other words, the agent's per-period value of information depends endogenously on his continuation value.
elements.sent-2411-033: In particular, a signal's value today is lower if the agent's continuation value from not purchasing it exceeds that from purchasing it.
elements.sent-2411-034: Indeed, when the gap between these two continuation values is sufficiently large, the agent's value for the signal becomes negative.
elements.sent-2411-035: A negative value of information plays a key role in determining the agent's ``bargaining power" in the repeated contracting game.
elements.sent-2411-036: When contracting with monopolist, the agent has limited bargaining power as he faces a take-it-or-leave-it offer from his only source of information.
elements.sent-2411-037: To gain leverage, the agent can refuse to purchase a signal unless it offers him a large enough share of the surplus in the current period.

elements.sent-2411-042: In other words, the equilibrium payoff set in a monopolistic setting has a bang-bang structure; depending on the search cost, either a unique equilibrium or a folk-theorem obtains.

elements.sent-2411-048: Using these characterizations, we compare the total surpluses and agent's payoffs generated in monopolistic and competitive settings, leading us to one of the following conclusions depending on the value of the search cost: $(i)$ monopoly and competition yield the same range of total surpluses and agent's payoffs, or $(ii)$ the highest total surplus generated under competition is no higher than the lowest total surplus generated under monopoly whereas the lowest agent's payoff generated under a competition is no lower than the highest agent's payoff generated under monopoly.
elements.sent-2411-049: Moreover, $(iii)$ for certain search cost values, competition yields a strictly higher agent's payoff but a strictly lower total surplus than does a monopoly.

elements.sent-2411-336: This paper studies the role competition in persuaded search markets where principal(s) dynamically contract with a searching agent who seeks information on the quality of sampled goods.
elements.sent-2411-337: We find that while competition benefits the agent, it does so, perhaps counterintuitively, at the expense of efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-338: These results are driven by the interaction between the value of information and bargaining power in dynamic markets.
elements.sent-2411-339: When the agent is facing a monopolist, he has bargaining power if and only if he can credibly reject a lackluster offer from the principal, which in turn holds if and only if he can have a negative value of information.
elements.sent-2411-340: In contrast, when facing multiple principals, the agent has bargaining power not just when he can credibly reject an offer by any one principal, but also when his value of that principal's signal is lower than his value for signals offered by other principals.
elements.sent-2411-341: This weaker condition allows the agent to secure a larger payoff in competitive settings than he could in a monopolistic one.
elements.sent-2411-342: However, while equilibrium outcomes under monopoly are not in general efficient, we show that the agent's stronger bargaining power in competitive settings can only decrease, and never increase, the total surplus generated across the set of equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-343: In an increasingly data-driven world, understanding the impact of competition in markets governing the sale of information is itself increasingly important.

elements.sent-2411-485: We formulate the principal-agent relationship as a repeated contracting problem within a stopping game, and characterize the set of equilibrium payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-486: For instance, between 2013 and 2016, CarFax was engaged in a class-action lawsuit wherein plaintiffs alleged that CarFax engaged in anti-competitive practices and had become a de facto monopoly, controlling 90\% of the market for certifying pre-owned vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-487: While CarFax admitted to its monopoly status, it argued that its use of short-term contracts (rather than long-term exclusive agreements) did not reduce welfare because it disciplined CarFax to always provide high quality information at a low price.\footnote{See details on the case Maxon Hyundai Mazda et al. v Carfax, Inc and the associated arguments referenced above at \url{https://casetext.com/case/mazda-v-carfax and \url{https://casetext.com/case/mazda-v-carfax-inc-1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-488: This paper explores the merit of such claims: how does competition, or lack thereof, among information brokers affect search outcomes, market efficiency, and the welfare of the searching agent? The answers to these questions are not a priori obvious.
elements.sent-2411-489: On one hand, a competitive setting might give rise to ``Bertrand-like" outcomes in which brokers provide more information and charge less for it than would a monopolist, leading to more efficient search and a higher payoff for the searching agent.
elements.sent-2411-490: On the other hand, competing brokers might collude.
elements.sent-2411-491: In our setting, collusion can occur along two dimensions: keeping the price of information high, or keeping informativeness low.
elements.sent-2411-492: Therefore, collusion between multiple brokers could instead lead to inefficient search outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-493: Moreover, there may be multiple equilibria that feature different ``levels of'' collusion.
elements.sent-2411-494: Thus, to satisfactorily answer the question of how competition affects search outcomes and welfare, one must compare across the set of equilibrium outcomes under various market structures.
elements.sent-2411-495: To this end, we embed a repeated contracting game between an agent and information brokers (henceforth, ``principals") into a canonical sequential search model \citep{mcc70 .}
elements.sent-2411-496: The agent then observes a signal realization based on his purchase decision, and decides either to terminate his search by matching with the good, or to discard the good and continue searching in the next period.
elements.sent-2411-497: We characterize the set of equilibrium payoffs in this persuaded search game, and study how the equilibrium payoff set varies between monopolistic and competitive settings.
elements.sent-2411-498: Should a regulator ``break up" a monopolist, like CarFax, into rival firms? Our results indicate the answer to this question depends on the goals of the regulator: If the regulator seeks to maximize efficiency, it will allow the monopolist to persist.
elements.sent-2411-499: In particular, a signal is less valuable today if the agent's continuation value from not purchasing it exceeds that from purchasing it.
elements.sent-2411-500: A negative value of information plays a key role in determining the agent's ``bargaining power" in the repeated contracting problem.
elements.sent-2411-501: Consider an agent who seeks to gain leverage by threatening to reject an offer that yields him too small a share of the surplus.
elements.sent-2411-502: Such a threat is credible only if he has a negative value of information in the present.
elements.sent-2411-503: In contrast, above the threshold, the agent's threats have no credibility.

elements.sent-2411-617: In an increasingly data-driven economy, however, information is acquired through endogenous contracting with brokers who gather, process, and sell data.
elements.sent-2411-618: Importantly, the value of the agent-broker relationship in the “upstream” information market is determined by how well it facilitates the agent’s decisions in the “downstream” search market.
elements.sent-2411-619: Examples of such persuaded search markets---where agents interact with an upstream information market and a downstream search market---include: labor markets in which employers contract with pre-employment testing agencies to assess job candidates; dating markets in which individuals pay for background checks on potential dates;\footnote{In a 2023 survey of U.S.}
elements.sent-2411-620: These settings share similarities in the downstream search market: agents typically have unit demand, and the search good either has no price or its price is often fixed in advance.\footnote{That is, the prices of search goods do not adjust based on purchased information.}
elements.sent-2411-621: For example, in labor markets, \cite{aut08 discuss how pre-employment test results typically affect only hiring decisions, not wages.}
elements.sent-2411-622: There are also similarities in the upstream market: information brokers seek to maximize profits and have no intrinsic preferences over an agent's outcome in the downstream market, and they face (near) zero marginal costs for providing more precise information.\footnote{For example, pre-employment testing agencies typically provide employers with coarse ``pass/fail" reports despite having more granular data on job candidates' test performance, suggesting that the marginal cost of a more informative test is negligible \citep{aut08 .}}
elements.sent-2411-623: To fix ideas, consider an agent searching for a used car.
elements.sent-2411-624: Increasingly, search is conducted through online marketplaces such as Carvana,\footnote{Carvana was the second-largest U.S.}
elements.sent-2411-625: Since direct inspection of cars is infeasible, the searching agent may turn to brokers like CarFax and AutoCheck, who aggregate and process data from law enforcement agencies, insurance companies, and auto-repair centers---data that would be prohibitively costly for an individual agent to acquire---into reports that the agent can purchase in order to learn about the quality of a car.
elements.sent-2411-626: Brokers determine how much information to disclose in these reports, thereby shaping the agent’s search.
elements.sent-2411-627: For example, if brokers provide uninformative reports, the agent has no way to distinguish the quality of cars, and so has little incentive to search.
elements.sent-2411-628: On the other hand, if brokers provide fully informative reports, then the agent will reject low-quality cars, thus leading to non-trivial search dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-629: Therefore, the brokers' supply of information plays a role in determining both how much surplus is created in the downstream search market, and their pricing decision determines how this surplus is divided between the agent and the brokers in the upstream market.

elements.sent-2411-813: An agent engages in sequential search and learns about the quality of sampled goods through signals purchased from profit-maximizing information broker(s).
elements.sent-2411-814: We study how the market structure---the number of competing brokers---shapes the pricing and design of information, as well as the resulting welfare outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-815: We characterize the equilibrium payoff set, and show that when the agent's search cost falls below a threshold, market structure affects neither how much surplus is generated in equilibrium nor how it is divided.
elements.sent-2411-816: Above this threshold, however, competition yields equilibrium outcomes that raise the agent's payoff but reduce total surplus relative to any monopoly equilibrium outcome.
elements.sent-2411-817: Methodologically, we extend the classic theory of repeated games to stopping problems, such as sequential search.
elements.sent-2411-818: Agents in search markets aim to find high-quality goods quickly but face a key hurdle: limited information about the quality of available goods.
elements.sent-2411-819: In an increasingly data-driven economy, however, agents may acquire this information by contracting with brokers who gather, process, and sell data.
elements.sent-2411-820: Examples of such persuaded search markets---where agents contract with ``upstream" information brokers to guide their decisions in a ``downstream" sequential search market---include: labor markets in which employers contract with pre-employment testing agencies to screen job candidates; dating markets in which individuals purchase background checks on potential dates; and goods markets where consumers rely on paid expert reviews to evaluate product quality.\footnote{ 82\% of US private-sector firms use pre-employment testing services to screen candidates (\url{https://www.sparcstart.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/2017-CandE-Report.pdf ).}}
elements.sent-2411-822: Similarly, 30\% of online shoppers rely on expert reviews to guide their purchase decisions (\url{https://www.powerreviews.com/reviews-influence-purchase-behavior-funnel/ ).}
elements.sent-2411-823: Increasingly, search takes place through online marketplaces such as Carvana,\footnote{Carvana was the second-largest U.S.}
elements.sent-2411-824: Thus, the searching agent may turn to brokers like CARFAX and AutoCheck, who aggregate and process data from law enforcement, insurance companies, and auto-repair centers into vehicle-quality reports that they often sell on a per-car basis via spot contracts.
elements.sent-2411-825: The value of such an agent–broker relationship is determined by how effectively it facilitates the agent’s decisions in the downstream search market.
elements.sent-2411-826: As the reports become more expensive---which reduces the agent's surplus---or less informative---diminishing the agent’s ability to distinguish high- from low-quality cars---the agent's incentive to search is also reduced.
elements.sent-2411-827: Therefore, the brokers' design and pricing of information jointly determine both the amount of surplus created in the downstream search market and how that surplus is divided between the agent and the brokers upstream.
elements.sent-2411-828: The dependence of the downstream search on the upstream contracting environment raises a natural question: How does competition, or lack thereof, among information brokers shape market efficiency and the welfare of the searching agent? To address this question, we embed a repeated contracting problem between an agent and a finite number of information brokers into a stopping game.
elements.sent-2411-829: The agent (``he") has unit demand for a good and attempts to fulfill this demand in a downstream market by engaging in sequential search, following the canonical framework of \citet{mcc70 .}
elements.sent-2411-830: However, unlike the standard model, he does not observe the good’s quality directly.
elements.sent-2411-831: Instead, he can acquire a signal about the quality of the sampled good from one of the brokers in the upstream information market.
elements.sent-2411-832: Each broker (``she") flexibly designs a signal and sells it through a take-it-or-leave-it spot contract.
elements.sent-2411-833: The agent buys one signal (if any), observes the corresponding signal realization, and then decides whether to stop and match with the sampled good or to continue searching in the next period.
elements.sent-2411-834: We characterize the set of equilibrium payoffs and compare both the total surplus and the agent’s payoff under monopoly and competition.
elements.sent-2411-835: When restricting attention to history-independent (stationary) strategies, we show that each market structure admits a unique equilibrium outcome.
elements.sent-2411-836: The agent's payoff is higher in a competitive setting than in a monopolistic one, echoing the classic result that Bertrand competition among sellers benefits the buyer.
elements.sent-2411-837: Moreover, we show that the market structure has no impact on total surplus, with both monopoly and competition leading to full efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-838: Yet focusing only on stationary equilibria is unduly restrictive, as it rules out plausible outcomes such as tacit collusion among brokers.\footnote{We do not model an official communication stage between brokers.}
elements.sent-2411-839: Nonetheless, ``tacit collusion'' arises when brokers coordinate on/select a broker-preferred equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-840: Alternatively, one can envision such coordination arising through explicit communication channels.
elements.sent-2411-841: Moreover, we show that for some parameter values, almost all equilibrium outcomes can only be sustained by non-stationary strategies.
elements.sent-2411-842: When we consider the full set of equilibria---arising both from stationary and non-stationary strategies---the comparison across monopoly and competition changes sharply: When search flow costs fall below a certain threshold, monopoly and competition yield the same range of total surpluses and agent payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-843: Above this threshold, however, the highest total surplus attainable under competition coincides with the lowest total surplus under monopoly, while the lowest agent payoff under competition is no smaller than the highest agent payoff under monopoly.
elements.sent-2411-844: Moreover, for some cost values above the threshold, nearly all competitive equilibria deliver strictly higher payoffs to the agent but strictly lower total surplus than does monopoly.
elements.sent-2411-845: In short, our main results establish that $(i)$ for low search costs, the creation and division of surplus is independent of market structure, while $(ii)$ for high search costs, competition benefits the agent but, perhaps counterintuitively, reduces efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-846: At the heart of our main results is the observation that the value of a signal to the agent depends on two distinct factors.
elements.sent-2411-847: The first is standard in any decision problem: a signal is valuable insofar as it helps the agent distinguish goods worth matching with from those that justify continued search.
elements.sent-2411-848: Since brokers design signals each period, they fully control this factor.
elements.sent-2411-849: The second factor is the agent’s continuation payoff.
elements.sent-2411-850: When the agent anticipates high future payoffs, he is less likely to stop searching today and thus has less need for information.
elements.sent-2411-851: This factor is unique to stopping games: because the agent has unit demand for the search good, he prefers to continue searching rather than match today and forgo a high continuation payoff.
elements.sent-2411-852: Consequently, a signal's value in a given period declines as the agent’s continuation payoff rises.
elements.sent-2411-853: Crucially, this dependence generates an endogenous bargaining power for the agent: he can credibly reject a contract that offers him a low payoff today if he expects to get a sufficiently high continuation payoff in future periods.\footnote{\cite{dana2011productquality study a related repeated sales game in which, in every period, a buyer purchases a good from sellers who choose both its price and quality.}}
elements.sent-2411-854: Because their setting is an infinitely repeated game rather than a stopping game, the consumption value of a good in the current period is independent of continuation values.
elements.sent-2411-855: They show that the buyer has bargaining power under monopoly if and only if he has it under competition.
elements.sent-2411-856: By contrast, in our persuaded search setting, the agent’s value of a signal in a given period depends directly on his continuation payoff.
elements.sent-2411-857: As our results will show, this distinction implies that the agent in our model has a strictly stronger bargaining position under competition than under monopoly---he has bargaining power under monopoly whenever he has it under a competitive market structure, but that the converse is not true.
elements.sent-2411-858: How does the agent's endogenous bargaining power differ by market structure? Consider first the monopolistic case with a single broker.
elements.sent-2411-859: The agent is naturally in a weak bargaining position because he faces a take-it-or-leave-it offer from his only source of information.
elements.sent-2411-860: He may attempt to gain leverage by threatening to reject unfavorable spot contracts in anticipation of high continuation values.
elements.sent-2411-861: In a stationary equilibrium, however, continuation values are fixed, rendering such threats non-credible.
elements.sent-2411-862: As we show, this lack of bargaining power results in a unique stationary equilibrium which features full surplus extraction---an efficient outcome that delivers the agent his lowest possible payoff.
elements.sent-2411-863: A richer set of outcomes arises under non-stationary equilibria, precisely because continuation payoffs can now be conditioned on the history of play.
elements.sent-2411-864: This flexibility can render the agent’s threats credible, thereby endogenously granting him bargaining power.
elements.sent-2411-865: Importantly, contracts offered by the broker are multidimensional, specifying both a price and a signal structure.
elements.sent-2411-866: Any bargaining power that sustains an efficient equilibrium yielding the agent a given expected payoff (e.g., the broker selling an informative signal at a high price) can equally sustain an inefficient one yielding him the same payoff (e.g., the broker selling a less informative signal at a lower price).

elements.sent-2411-868: Next, consider a competitive market structure with multiple brokers.
elements.sent-2411-869: The unique stationary equilibrium outcome here features Bertrand competition: in each period, at least two brokers offer fully informative signals for free.
elements.sent-2411-870: Similar to the stationary equilibrium under a monopoly, this outcome is efficient, but unlike the monopolistic case, the agent now receives the entire surplus.
elements.sent-2411-871: When we move beyond stationary strategies in competitive settings, brokers may tacitly collude to recover some of the surplus they would otherwise compete away.
elements.sent-2411-872: Yet the agent can blunt such collusion by once again threatening to reject contracts that yield him too little surplus.
elements.sent-2411-873: Relative to monopoly, however, competition yields the agent an advantage: rejecting one broker’s contract does not preclude obtaining information in the current period, since the agent can instead accept another broker’s offer.
elements.sent-2411-874: In this way, competition provides the agent with an ``inside option'' that amplifies his endogenous bargaining power.
elements.sent-2411-875: As a result, competition more readily sustains equilibria that yield high agent payoffs as well as inefficient outcomes.

elements.sent-2411-975: This paper studies the role of competition in persuaded search markets, where an agent engaged in sequential search acquires information on the quality of sampled goods by repeatedly purchasing signals from a finite set of information brokers.
elements.sent-2411-976: We show that greater competition among brokers expands the set of equilibria in ways that raise the agent’s payoff but, perhaps counterintuitively, reduces total surplus.
elements.sent-2411-977: These results stem from the interaction between competition and bargaining power in brokered search markets, where the agent's continuation value directly affects both his probability of terminating his search and also his willingness to pay for information.
elements.sent-2411-978: Under a monopoly, the agent has bargaining power if and only if he can credibly reject a lackluster offer from the broker, which in turn holds if and only if he is willing to forego an informative signal today in order to induce more informative or cheaper signals in future periods.
elements.sent-2411-979: Under competition, however, rejecting any one broker’s offer does not require foregoing information in the current period; the agent can instead acquire information from another competing broker.
elements.sent-2411-980: Competition thus provides the agent with an inside option, strengthening his bargaining position relative to the monopolistic case.
elements.sent-2411-981: This allows the agent to capture more of the surplus, but also sustains inefficient outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-982: From an applied perspective, our welfare results highlight a non-trivial policy tradeoff: whether a regulator should “break up” a monopolist into rival brokers depends on whether the objective is to enhance the agent’s welfare or to maximize efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-983: Such nuanced policy implications echo recent legal decisions.
elements.sent-2411-984: Between 2013 and 2016, CARFAX was engaged in a class-action lawsuit wherein plaintiffs alleged that CARFAX engaged in anti-competitive practices and had become a de facto monopoly, controlling 90\% of the market for certifying pre-owned vehicles.
